serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of dry pork bee hoon weighs approximately 300 g, representing a typical single-person serving as commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice Vermicelli with Pork (Bee Hoon Goreng Babi) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo does not carry a direct entry for 'Dry Pork Bee Hoon' as a named dish. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entries for fried bee hoon with pork and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked rice vermicelli (NDB 20050) and lean pork (NDB 10006) to construct a weighted per-100 g composite. Proportions estimated as approximately 55% cooked rice vermicelli, 30% cooked pork, 10% vegetables and aromatics, 5% oil and sauces. Energy cross-checked against published Singapore hawker food nutrient tables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dry pork bee hoon as a composite dish. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli (boiled), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Atkins et al. (2008) Diabetes Care report GI of 53 (±6) for rice vermicelli. The addition of pork, oil, and soy sauce is expected to lower the effective GI slightly due to fat and protein co-ingestion; GI of 53 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving calculated as (53 × 61.5 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 32.7.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and dark soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (1,260 mg per standard 300 g serving), this dish contributes more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index rice vermicelli base with significant carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 53 and a glycemic load of ~32.7 per standard serving, this dish produces a moderate-to-high postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork and sodium from soy-based sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 1.9 g saturated fat and 420 mg sodium per 100 g presents a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean pork cuts, request light soy sauce, and limit consumption to occasional servings within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Pork Bee Hoon is a Singaporean stir-fried rice vermicelli dish featuring thin, translucent rice noodles (bee hoon or mee hoon) tossed in a wok with sliced or minced pork, garlic, spring onions, bean sprouts, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil. The 'dry' preparation distinguishes it from soupy bee hoon variants: the noodles are cooked until the sauce is fully absorbed, yielding a slightly caramelised, savoury coating rather than a broth. The texture is soft yet slightly chewy from the rice vermicelli, with tender pork pieces providing protein and umami depth. Culturally, bee hoon dishes are a staple of Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or dinner. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 504 kcal, 27.6 g protein, 17.4 g fat, and 61.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is notable for its high sodium content (~1,260 mg per serving) due to soy-based sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the rice vermicelli base is approximately 53 (low-to-moderate), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~32.7), requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content and is not vegetarian or vegan.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for 'Dry Pork Bee Hoon' as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo fried bee hoon entries and USDA SR Legacy component data using a defensible ingredient proportion model. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli (boiled). Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty in exact macro and micronutrient values.
notes: 1. The input record tagged this dish as 'Thai' cuisine; however, bee hoon (rice vermicelli) dry pork is a well-established Singaporean hawker dish. The cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean' with region_of_origin 'Singapore'. The Thai cuisine tag in the original field survey listing likely reflects the restaurant's broader menu category rather than the dish's actual culinary origin. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients under standard preparation. 4. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. The is_halal flag is set to false. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the composite dish was found in approved sources. 6. Sodium content is high due to soy-based sauces; the exact sodium level will vary by restaurant preparation and sauce quantity. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and all micronutrients except vitamin_d_mcg are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are therefore approximate.
